name: Matching Stack
summary: Push each opening bracket; on a closer, pop the top and check it is the matching opener.
visualizationIntent: init: empty stack, ready to scan the string left to right. loop: read the current character c. check: if c is a closing bracket, look at the stack — an empty stack means no opener to match (invalid), otherwise pop the top opener into 'top' and compare it to the opener that c requires (pairs[c]); a mismatch is invalid. update: if c is an opening bracket, push it onto the stack (it must be matched later). return: after the whole string is scanned, a stack that is empty means every opener was matched -> valid (true), a non-empty stack means unmatched openers remain -> invalid (false).

lineExplanations.3: Start with an empty stack that will hold opening brackets still waiting to be closed.
lineExplanations.4: Build a lookup mapping each closing bracket to the opening bracket that must match it.
lineExplanations.5: Scan the string one character at a time, left to right.
lineExplanations.6: Check whether the current character is a closing bracket (the closers are the keys of pairs).
lineExplanations.7: It is a closer, so check whether the stack is empty — if so there is no opener to match.
lineExplanations.8: The stack was empty, so this closer has no partner: mark the string invalid.
lineExplanations.9: Return False right away — one unmatched closer makes the whole string invalid.
lineExplanations.10: Pop the most recently opened bracket off the top of the stack to match against this closer.
lineExplanations.11: Compare the popped opener with the opener this closer actually requires.
lineExplanations.12: They differ, so the brackets are mismatched: mark the string invalid.
lineExplanations.13: Return False right away — a single mismatch makes the whole string invalid.
lineExplanations.15: The character is an opening bracket, so push it onto the stack to be matched later.
lineExplanations.16: After the whole string is scanned, the string is valid only if the stack is empty.
lineExplanations.17: Return the final verdict: True if every opener was matched, False otherwise.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: is the class wrapper the platform expects around the method.
syntaxExplanations.2: def isValid(self, s: str) -> bool: defines the method; it receives the string s and returns a boolean.
syntaxExplanations.3: stack = [] creates an empty Python list used as a stack (.append pushes, .pop removes the last item).
syntaxExplanations.4: { ')': '(', ... } is a dict literal mapping each closing character to its matching opening character.
syntaxExplanations.5: for c in s: loops over the string, binding c to each character in turn.
syntaxExplanations.6: if c in pairs: is a membership test — True when c is one of the dict's keys (the closing brackets).
syntaxExplanations.7: if not stack: uses that an empty list is falsy, so 'not stack' is True exactly when the stack is empty.
syntaxExplanations.8: result = False assigns the boolean False to result.
syntaxExplanations.9: return result hands the value back to the caller and exits the function immediately.
syntaxExplanations.10: stack.pop() removes and returns the last item of the list — the top of the stack.
syntaxExplanations.11: != tests inequality; pairs[c] looks up the opener that the closing bracket c requires.
syntaxExplanations.12: result = False assigns the boolean False to result.
syntaxExplanations.13: return result exits the function immediately with the current verdict.
syntaxExplanations.14: else: introduces the branch taken when the 'if c in pairs' test was False (c is an opener).
syntaxExplanations.15: stack.append(c) adds c to the end of the list — pushing it onto the stack.
syntaxExplanations.16: not stack is True when the stack is empty; the result is assigned to result.
syntaxExplanations.17: return result returns the final boolean verdict.
